passage.title: Built for the Night
passage.subject: ELA
passage.sourceName: Authored for PSSA Grade 3 paired-passage stamina pilot
passage.text:
Owls are birds that do much of their hunting after sunset. Their bodies are built for the dark. An owl's eyes are large and face forward, so the bird can judge distance when it dives toward prey. The eyes do not roll from side to side the way human eyes do. To look around, an owl turns its whole head instead. This head turning may look strange, but it solves a real problem. The owl can keep its sharp eyes aimed forward and still check the space around a branch, field, or fence post.

An owl also listens in a special way. In many owls, one ear opening sits a little higher than the other. A sound reaches each ear at a slightly different time. That tiny difference helps the owl tell whether a rustling mouse is above, below, left, or right. The circle of feathers around the face works almost like a dish. It guides sound toward the hidden ears. When the owl hears a squeak under grass or leaves, the sound pattern helps the bird aim before it drops.

Silent flight is another night-hunting tool. The front edges of many owl feathers are comb-like instead of smooth. Soft fringes on the wings help break up air as the bird flaps. Because the feathers quiet the wingbeats, a mouse may not hear the owl coming until the owl is very close. The quiet flight does not make the owl invisible, but it gives the hunter a few extra seconds. In a dark field, those seconds can matter.

These features work together. Big forward-facing eyes help the owl see in dim light. Uneven ears and a face shaped for hearing help it aim at tiny sounds. Quiet wings let it move without warning the animals below. None of the features works alone like magic. Together, they make the owl a careful night hunter.

factCheckNotesJson.claim: Owls have forward-facing eyes that are fixed in their sockets, so they turn their heads to look around.
Many owls use asymmetrical ear placement to locate sound.
An owl's facial disk helps direct sound toward the ears.
Comb-like and fringed wing feathers help owls fly quietly.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: Evolution and Ecology of Silent Flight in Owls and Other Flying Vertebrates

passage.title: The Barn's Best Helper
passage.subject: ELA
passage.sourceName: Authored for PSSA Grade 3 paired-passage stamina pilot
passage.text:
A barn owl may look pale and quiet, but it can be a busy helper on a farm. Barn owls hunt mostly small mammals such as mice, voles, and rats. A nesting pair with hungry young can catch many rodents in one season. That matters to farmers because rodents can chew stored grain, damage fields, and carry disease. When fewer rodents reach a storage shed or field, the farmer may lose less food and spend less time fixing damage.

Some farms put up nest boxes to invite barn owls to live nearby. A nest box gives the owls a safe place to raise young when old barns or hollow trees are hard to find. If the land around the box has enough grass and field edges for hunting, the owls may stay and search the farm at night. The box does not feed the owls by itself. It simply gives them a home base near the places where mice and voles move.

Barn owls are not a perfect answer to every pest problem. They are wild animals, not workers that can be ordered around. A farm still needs clean storage, careful planting, and other smart choices. But an owl family can reduce the number of rodents without leaving poison in the field. That makes barn owls one part of a safer pest plan, especially on farms that already have good hunting habitat.

Poison can create a different problem. If a poisoned mouse staggers into the open, an owl, hawk, fox, dog, or cat may eat it. The poison can then harm the animal that ate the mouse. For that reason, many wildlife groups warn people to be careful with rodent poisons and to protect natural hunters when possible. Helping barn owls is not only about liking birds. It is also about choosing a method that can protect crops while causing less harm to other animals.

factCheckNotesJson.claim: Barn owls feed heavily on rodents and other small mammals.
Barn owls feed mainly on small mammals such as rats, mice, and voles, and a nesting pair brings large amounts of prey to the nest to feed their young.
Nest boxes are used to encourage barn owls in suitable habitat.
Rodent poison can harm predators and pets that eat poisoned rodents.

items.subject: ELA
items.stem: Use details from both passages to explain why owls can be helpful hunters.

scoreBandExamples.response: Owls are helpful hunters because their eyes, ears, face feathers, and quiet wings help them catch prey at night. Barn owls can then help farms by catching rodents that damage grain and fields.
Owls hunt quietly at night and barn owls eat rodents on farms.
scoreBandExamples.why: Uses both passages with less explanation.
Uses one relevant idea but not both passages.
Does not answer with support from the paired passages.

items.studentFacingPrompt: Use details from both passages to explain why owls can be helpful hunters.

correctResponseJson.expectedAnswerCore: Owls are helpful hunters because their bodies are built to catch prey and because what they catch helps people. The first passage explains that an owl's forward-facing eyes help it judge distance when it dives, and its offset ears help it locate prey, so it hunts well in the dark. The second passage explains that barn owls catch rodents such as mice and rats that would damage a farmer's grain and crops, so the owls help farmers lose less food.

acceptableTextSupport.quotedSpan: Barn owls hunt mostly small mammals such as mice, voles, and rats.
acceptableTextSupport.detail: owls catch rodents that damage crops and grain
